techniques.evidence_quote: the intrusion began in the customer's cloud environment, where the group gained access to an IT administrator's account, via Okta single sign-on (SSO)
the intrusion was attributed to a social-engineering attack, in which the user's credentials were reset by the attackers
the IT infrastructure architect was observed configuring Okta with a secondary identity provider (IdP)... the exact event that allows cross-tenant Okta impersonation of a privileged user
To maintain persistence, the group used RMM and reverse proxy solutions; use of Ngrok was shortly followed by a URL request to retrieve Ngrok keys from paste.ee
PDQConnectAgent, ScreenConnect, fleet.io, rsocx

IT infrastructure architect authenticating and passing MFA from the same malicious IP address (99.25.84[.]9). The IT infrastructure architect also served as the organization's virtualization engineer and had a highly privileged account.
sysadminanywhere.exe (privilege escalation)
WindowsDefenderATPOffboardingPackage_valid_until_2023-XX-XX.zip (defense evasion)
Forensia.exe (defense evasion)... BleachBit.exe (defense evasion)
the adversary deleted some CyberArk files for vCenter-related credentials, seemingly in an attempt to disrupt access to the hosts after earlier actions
the group used a US-based IP address not connected to VPN infrastructure. We believe the threat actor exhibited a strong sense of operational security, used to evade typical rules
the group conducted an [MFA fatigue attack], attempting four MFA challenges within two minutes. The last challenge resulted in successful authentication
Once Scattered Spider gained access to the account, the group enrolled a new MFA device.
the threat actor had attempted to access the company's LastPass page... We are moderately confident that Scattered Spider managed to gain access to the customer's LastPass Vault.
gosecretsdump_win_v0.3.1.exe (credential access)
checking out CyberArk credentials for the VMWareVCenterSharedCreds folder
several file-access events indicated file and directory discovery in the organization's SharePoint platform

Scattered Spider hijacked active Citrix VDI sessions on the host GenericCitrixAPPServer.CUSTOMER.com to perform Active Directory (AD) discovery... the group downloaded AD Explorer from the Sysinternals website and executed it
Performing Azure AD user, group, onPremSync, PIM role, enumeration queries
Configuring Azure API access... Enumerating Azure AD Permission
Enumerating Azure AD Permission... Performing Azure AD user, group, onPremSync, PIM role, enumeration queries
Scattered Spider authenticated to Citrix Workspace via the IT administrator's Okta SSO credentials
We observed the use of RDP for lateral movement to additional hosts. We saw explicit logins using the credentials of two compromised service accounts
Scattered Spider hijacked active Citrix VDI sessions on the host GenericCitrixAPPServer.CUSTOMER.com
The group used CyberArk access for lateral movement, which included SSH access to vCenter hosts, such as CUSTOMERvCenter100.
The compromised user within the VDI accessed a file located in the organization's AWS S3 bucket, specifically at: customer.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/lastpass_export%20cleaned.xlsx
use of Ngrok was shortly followed by a URL request to retrieve Ngrok keys from paste.ee. Sandboxing the webpage, while it was still up, showed Ngrok authentication tokens being hosted.
a URL request to retrieve Ngrok keys from paste.ee
PDQConnectAgent, ScreenConnect, fleet.io, rsocx
Exfiltration was observed via the IP address 144.76.136[.]153... The exfiltration domain transfer.sh is associated with this IP address.
suspicious commands being pushed to vCenter servers that included reverting hosts to their latest snapshot and deleting all snapshots
the attackers successfully accomplished their objectives of data exfiltration and widespread encryption
